calibration.rationale: Thresholds are set at the 60th–70th percentile of activation observed in our demo result corpus and the test stubs. They are deliberately conservative — high precision, lower recall — until we accumulate a real-world corpus and run scripts/calibrate_patterns.py to recalibrate.

dropped_patterns.reason: The originally-proposed inverse pattern (high Gut Reaction + low Brain Effort = 'pure feeling, no thinking') has no published support for the dlPFC-suppression half. Critchley et al. 2004 establishes anterior insula as the cortical seat of interoceptive awareness, but does not show that high insula co-occurs with low dlPFC, nor that this combination indexes 'feeling without thinking.' If anything, the emotion-regulation literature (Ochsner & Gross 2005) suggests dlPFC up-regulates to dampen affect — the opposite anti-correlation. Dropped from v1 rather than ship a stretch.

patterns.name: Learning Moment
patterns.short_desc: The cortex is encoding this moment into long-term memory.
patterns.long_desc: When the dorsal attention network (DAN) and left ventrolateral prefrontal cortex (vlPFC) co-activate, the cortex is doing the work that predicts later recall. This is the 'subsequent memory effect' — researchers showed in 1998 that brain activity *during* encoding predicts which moments a viewer will remember days later, while activity that's absent at this moment is forgotten. Attention orients the cortex to the moment; vlPFC binds it into memory.

citations.authors: Wagner, Schacter, Rotte, Koutstaal, Maril, Dale, Rosen, Buckner
citations.title: Building memories: remembering and forgetting of verbal experiences as predicted by brain activity
citations.claim: Foundational subsequent-memory-effect paper. Left vlPFC activity at encoding predicts later remembered vs forgotten words.

citations.title: Control of goal-directed and stimulus-driven attention in the brain
citations.claim: Defines the dorsal attention network (DAN) as the system that orients attention to task-relevant content.

citations.title: Imaging cognition II: An empirical review of 275 PET and fMRI studies
citations.claim: Meta-analysis providing the broader functional-anatomy backdrop. Cite as background, not for SME specifically.

patterns.citation_summary: The 'subsequent memory effect' — activity during encoding predicts which moments will later be remembered.

patterns.name: Emotional Impact
patterns.short_desc: The viewer is feeling this moment as personally meaningful.
patterns.long_desc: When medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) and anterior insula co-activate, two systems engage at once. mPFC tracks self-relevance and subjective value — 'is this about me, do I care about it?'. The anterior insula tracks interoception — the felt, bodily edge of experience. Together they index a moment that lands as *personally felt*. Falk and colleagues showed in 2012 that mPFC activation during anti-smoking PSAs predicted real-world quit-line call volume at the population level, which makes this combination a documented predictor of real behaviour change in one specific domain.

citations.title: Cortical midline structures and the self
citations.claim: Establishes mPFC and adjacent midline regions as the cortical seat of self-referential processing.

citations.title: How do you feel — now? The anterior insula and human awareness
citations.claim: Anterior insula as the cortical hub for interoceptive awareness — the felt, bodily edge of emotion.

citations.title: The valuation system: A coordinate-based meta-analysis of BOLD fMRI experiments examining neural correlates of subjective value
citations.claim: Meta-analysis (~200 studies) showing ventromedial PFC tracks subjective value — formalises the 'is it worth caring about' computation that anchors this pattern.

citations.title: From neural responses to population behavior: neural focus group predicts population-level media effects
citations.claim: mPFC response to anti-smoking PSAs predicted population-level call-volume changes. Documented behavioural prediction in one specific domain (health PSAs).
citations.scope_caveat: Small sample (~30), one domain (health PSAs). Don't overgeneralise to all emotional content.

patterns.citation_summary: mPFC tracks self-relevance + subjective value; anterior insula tracks felt bodily response. Together: the moment lands personally and viscerally.

patterns.name: Reasoning Beat
patterns.short_desc: The viewer is actively working through what the content means.
patterns.long_desc: When dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC) and the language-selective network (Broca's area + Wernicke's area) co-activate, the viewer is doing effortful semantic processing — pulling structure and meaning out of the content rather than just letting it wash over them. dlPFC scales reliably with cognitive load (Owen et al.'s n-back meta-analysis); the language network scales with linguistic complexity (Fedorenko et al.). Co-activation = both load and language are high, which characterises moments where the audience is actually thinking.

citations.title: N-back working memory paradigm: a meta-analysis of normative functional neuroimaging studies
citations.claim: ALE meta-analysis establishing that dlPFC activation reliably tracks working-memory load.

citations.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
citations.title: Functional specificity for high-level linguistic processing in the human brain
citations.claim: Language-selective network (Broca + Wernicke) responds selectively to sentence structure and scales with linguistic complexity.

citations.title: New method for fMRI investigations of language: defining ROIs functionally in individual subjects
citations.claim: Methodological foundation: how the language network is identified per-subject. Cited for the ROI definition itself.

patterns.citation_summary: dlPFC scales with effort; the language network scales with linguistic richness. Co-activation = active reasoning, not passive consumption.

patterns.name: Social Resonance
patterns.short_desc: The viewer is reading the people on screen as relatable, not abstract.
patterns.long_desc: When the right temporoparietal junction (rTPJ) and medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) co-activate, the viewer is doing two things at once: inferring mental states of others (rTPJ) and routing those inferences through self-relevant evaluation (mPFC). Saxe & Kanwisher (2003) established rTPJ as selective for theory-of-mind. Mitchell, Macrae & Banaji (2006) showed mPFC engages differently when judging others perceived as similar to the self vs dissimilar — wiring self-processing to social cognition through the same region. Co-activation here = the audience is treating the people on screen as relatable, not abstract.

citations.title: People thinking about thinking people: the role of the temporo-parietal junction in 'theory of mind'
citations.claim: Right TPJ activates selectively when attributing mental states to others, beyond general social/biological-motion processing.

citations.title: Dissociable medial prefrontal contributions to judgments of similar and dissimilar others
citations.claim: Ventral mPFC engages for similar others, dorsal mPFC for dissimilar others — links self-processing to social cognition through the same region.

patterns.citation_summary: TPJ for inferring others' minds; mPFC for routing those inferences through self-relevance. Together: the audience is connecting, not observing.
